content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Part 1 of the “Frontend Performance That Users Feel” series. One innocent import added 300 KB. Nobody noticed for a year.
The app feels fast on the developer’s machine — fast CPU, fast network, everything cached. Then a real user on a mid-range phone over spotty mobile data taps the link and stares at a blank white screen for six seconds while 2 megabytes of JavaScript download and parse. They leave before your app even renders.
The cruel part: every individual decision that led there was reasonable. “We need a date library.” “This component library is convenient.” “Let’s add charts.” Each npm install is small in isolation, and nobody's watching the total. That's the bundle size trap — death by a thousand imports, invisible until you measure it.
Users don’t experience your code. They experience your bundle — how many bytes their device has to download, parse, and execute before anything works. That number is a product metric, and most teams never look at it.
First: See the Number
You cannot manage what you don’t measure, and almost nobody measures their bundle. Start here:
# See exactly what's in your bundle, by module
npx vite-bundle-visualizer # Vite
# or
npx webpack-bundle-analyzer # WebpackThis renders a treemap of your bundle. The first time you run it is usually a shock: a single date library is 70 KB, a UI kit you used two components from shipped all 400, and an icon package included 10,000 icons so you could use six. The waste is concrete and visible — and now fixable.
Fix #1: Import Only What You Use
The most common bloat is importing a whole library to use one function.
// Ships the ENTIRE library (~70 KB) even though you use one function
import _ from "lodash";
_.debounce(fn, 300);// Ships only debounce (~2 KB)
import debounce from "lodash/debounce";// Or skip the dep entirely — many "utilities" are a few lines now
const debounce = (fn, ms) => { let t; return (...a) => { clearTimeout(t); t = setTimeout(() => fn(...a), ms); }; };Check that your imports are tree-shakeable (named imports from ES-module packages) and prefer modern, smaller alternatives: date-fns over moment, native Intl over formatting libraries, native fetch over axios where you can.
Fix #2: Split the Bundle by Route and Interaction
Your users don’t need the code for the settings page to view the homepage. Code-split so each route loads only its own JavaScript:
// Instead of importing every page up front...
import Settings from "./Settings";// ...load it lazily, only when the user navigates there
const Settings = lazy(() => import("./Settings"));The same applies to heavy components behind an interaction — a chart library, a rich text editor, a modal. Load them when the user actually opens the thing, not on first paint. This turns one giant initial download into a small core plus on-demand chunks.
Fix #3: Defer the Code You Don’t Need Immediately
Not everything needs to block the first render. Analytics, chat widgets, A/B testing scripts, and other third-party tags are notorious for bloating the critical path:

• Load third-party scripts with async/defer so they don't block parsing.

• Mount non-critical widgets (support chat, cookie banners) after the main content is interactive.

• Audit third-party scripts ruthlessly — they’re often the biggest, least-owned part of your bundle, and each one is someone else’s code running on your users’ devices.
Fix #4: Set a Budget and Guard It in CI
The trap reopens the moment you stop watching. Make the bundle size a number CI enforces:
// Fail the build if the main bundle exceeds the budget
// (size-limit, bundlesize, or your bundler's performance budget)
{
"size-limit": [{ "path": "dist/main.js", "limit": "180 KB" }]
}Now a PR that adds 300 KB gets flagged in review instead of discovered a year later by an analytics dashboard showing mobile users bouncing. The budget turns “someone should keep an eye on this” into “the build won’t let it regress.”
Putting It Together

• Measure with a bundle analyzer — see the treemap, find the bloat.

• Import precisely — tree-shakeable, named imports; smaller alternatives; sometimes no dep at all.

• Code-split by route and heavy interaction so the initial load is small.

• Defer third-party and non-critical scripts off the critical path.

• Budget in CI so the bundle can’t silently balloon again.
The Takeaway
Bundle size is the performance metric that hides best, because it grows one reasonable import at a time and never looks broken on a developer’s fast machine. But your users feel every kilobyte on their real devices and real networks — as blank screens, delayed interactivity, and silent bounces. Measure the number, cut the obvious waste, split what you can defer, and guard it with a budget. The app that feels instant for everyone, not just for you, is almost always the one whose team actually watches its bundle.

This is Part 1 of the Frontend Performance That Users Feel series. Next up: why your React component re-renders 50 times when it should render twice.
What’s the biggest single thing you ever cut from a bundle? 👇
